glossaryTerms.definition: An internal skeleton, usually wire or foil, that supports clay during building and keeps extended forms from collapsing under their own weight.
Clay mixed with paper fibre. The fibre reinforces the dried body and reduces cracking, making it better than standard air-dry clay for thin-walled or extended forms.
Scratching the surface of clay with a needle tool or serrated tool before joining. Scoring creates a rough surface that grips slip and prevents joins from separating.
Liquid clay, used as an adhesive when joining two clay surfaces. Made by mixing clay with water to a thick cream consistency.
Coating a finished air-dry clay piece with undiluted PVA (polyvinyl acetate) glue. PVA dries clear and hard, adding surface strength and light moisture resistance.

content.text: Standard air-dry clay cannot support outstretched arms, thin legs, or any extended form much longer than about 5 cm without cracking or collapsing during drying. An
armature
solves this: a simple skeleton of thin florist wire bent to the rough shape of a standing figure, onto which
paper clay

is pressed and built up in sections. Paper clay air-dry is better than standard air-dry clay for this because the paper fibre reduces shrinkage cracking and allows thinner walls that dry around the wire without splitting. The finished figurine is sealed with PVA for surface hardness. This project builds a simple standing figure about 15 cm tall.

materials.name: Aluminium foil (for bulk filling body sections)
Slip (paper clay mixed with water to a cream)

tools.name: Wire cutters or strong scissors (for cutting wire)

content.text: Preparation

Sketch the figurine on paper first: a basic standing pose with arms at the sides or slightly raised. Mark approximate lengths for the torso, legs, arms, and head. These measurements guide the wire bending. For a 15 cm figurine: legs roughly 7 cm each, torso 5 cm, head 3 cm, arms 4 cm each. The wire runs continuously through the body - no joins.
Make
slip
before starting: mix a small amount of paper clay with water until smooth and thick, like double cream. Keep the jar covered with a damp cloth while working.
Steps

Cut two lengths of wire: one long piece (about 35 cm) for the spine and legs, and one shorter piece (about 25 cm) for the arms. Fold the long piece in half to form the two legs, leaving a looped top at the crotch point. Twist the two strands together from crotch to shoulder to form the spine (about 5 cm of twist).

Thread the arm wire horizontally through the twisted spine at shoulder height. Bend the ends out to each side to form the arms. Splay the feet wires into a small V shape at the base - this gives the figure a wider, more stable footprint.
Test the armature for balance by standing it on a flat surface. Adjust the leg angles until it stands without help. This is much easier to fix now than after clay is applied.

Build the body core. If using foil, crumple a small piece around the torso section of the wire to bulk it out before adding clay. This reduces clay weight and drying time. Keep foil bulk to roughly 60% of the intended final volume.

Apply clay to the torso first. Take small pieces (2-3 cm diameter) of paper clay and press them onto the wire/foil core, squeezing them firmly so the clay grips the wire. Build up in layers, smoothing each addition with a damp finger or the pottery sponge. Keep wall thickness to 5-8 mm.

Build the legs next. Roll thin sausages of clay (about 8 mm diameter) and press them around each leg wire, smoothing the joins at the hip. Build the feet as slightly flattened ovals at the base of each leg.
Build the arms. Apply clay around each arm wire in the same way. For arms held close to the body, allow them to overlap slightly with the torso and use
scoring
and slip at the join to prevent separation during drying.

Build the head. Form a small ball of clay (roughly 3 cm diameter) for the head. Pierce a hole through the centre with the needle tool, thread it onto the top of the spine wire, and press firmly to seat it. Smooth the neck join with a damp finger. Add minimal facial features at this stage if wanted.

Let the figure dry in stages: leave for 4-6 hours, check for any cracks opening at joins, and gently press them closed while the clay is still slightly soft. Use a dab of slip on any crack that needs filling.
Drying and finishing
Allow 48-3 days for full drying. The figurine can be stood upright during drying since the wire holds it. Sand any rough patches with fine sandpaper once bone-dry. Apply two coats of undiluted
PVA sealing
, letting each coat dry fully. The PVA hardens the surface and protects it from light knocks. Paint with acrylics before the final PVA coat for a sealed colour finish.
Variations
Make a seated figure by bending the leg wires at 90 degrees at the hip before applying clay.
Build a small animal by adjusting the armature to four legs with a longer horizontal spine.
Add texture to clothing areas using a comb or the scoring tool pressed into soft clay before it firms.
Mount the finished figure on a small clay base tile for a stable display platform.

items.problem: Crack opened at the shoulder/arm join during drying
items.cause: The arm clay was not scored and slipped to the torso
items.fix: While still slightly soft, press slip into the crack and smooth. Score both surfaces first if re-joining detached pieces.

items.problem: Figurine leans to one side after drying
items.cause: Leg wire angles were uneven and clay weight pulled the lighter side
items.fix: Straighten the armature before applying clay. If already dry, glue a small clay base under the short leg to level it.

items.problem: Wire showing through the clay surface
items.cause: Clay layer was applied too thin over the wire
items.fix: Apply another thin layer of fresh clay over the area, blended in with a damp sponge.

items.cause: The neck join was not secured with scoring and slip
items.fix: Reattach with PVA glue once fully dry, or with fresh slip if still slightly damp.
